findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact insertion point in `_apply_mutations`: since there is no top-level `if not persist` guard, specify that the implementor should add `if persist: check_store_compat(...)` immediately after `persist = ctx.persist` is bound (line ~787), before the filter/preflight logic.
evidence: reconcile.py:776-820: _apply_mutations reads ctx.persist at line 787 but has no 'if not persist: return' guard at its entry
Plan states: 'Gate there, after the persist=True check (so dry-run / reconcile-check / cap-0 no-write passes are excluded) and before applier.apply()'
The persist check used to gate applier.apply is at reconcile.py:857 inside a try block in _apply_mutations, not a top-level guard
findings.finding: The plan says to insert the gate in _apply_mutations 'after the persist=True check', but _apply_mutations() has NO top-level early-return check for `not persist` — the persist gating lives inside applier.apply() downstream. The insertion point description is ambiguous and may cause the implementor to look for a guard that does not exist at the function entry.
findings.location: What section / 'Two paths do NOT go through lock.acquire()' bullet 2 / Scope section reconcile.py bullet
scenarios: Implementor looks for an 'if not persist' early-return in _apply_mutations to know where to insert the check_store_compat call, finds none, and inserts it at the wrong location or must add their own persist guard.
Gate inserted before the persist check would incorrectly also fire on dry-run / reconcile-check passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'after the persist=True check' with: 'add `if persist: check_store_compat(repo_root / ".tickets-tracker")` immediately after `persist = ctx.persist` is assigned in `_apply_mutations` (before the filter narrowing block)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the `run_ensures` broad exception handler masking `StoreIncompatibleError`: either (a) re-raise `StoreIncompatibleError` explicitly before the broad `except Exception` block in `run_ensures`, or (b) do not route the compat gate through `lock.acquire()` for the ensure-sweep path — instead call `check_store_compat()` explicitly in `init_cli`/MCP-boot entry points before `run_ensures`, so the gate fires at the right layer without being swallowed.
evidence: ensures.py line 159-162: `except LockTimeout as exc: logger.warning(...) / except Exception as exc: logger.warning(...)` — both handlers return normally, treating any exception from write_lock() as a non-fatal sweep skip

The plan inserts check_store_compat() inside lock.acquire(), which is called by write_lock(), which is called by run_ensures(); StoreIncompatibleError is an Exception subclass and will be caught by the outer handler

Plan 'Expand phase' section: 'a missed sweep never blocks writes — it only defers stamping' — but this logic applies only to LockTimeout, not to StoreIncompatibleError; conflating them means an incompatible store is treated as a contended lock
findings.finding: The `run_ensures` function's broad `except Exception` handler (ensures.py:161) will silently swallow `StoreIncompatibleError` raised by `check_store_compat()` inside `lock.acquire()`, defeating the fail-closed gate for any caller that goes through `run_ensures` (MCP-boot, init, fsck --repair). When the compat record is corrupt or incompatible, `run_ensures` logs a warning and returns normally instead of propagating the gate error — so init and MCP-boot continue without being blocked, violating the fail-closed guarantee for those entry points.
findings.location: src/rebar/_store/ensures.py:159-162 (the `except Exception` handler that catches LockTimeout and all other exceptions from `write_lock()`)
scenarios: A store has a corrupt .store-compat.json. User runs `rebar init` or MCP boots. run_ensures() calls write_lock(), which calls lock.acquire(), which calls check_store_compat() and raises StoreIncompatibleError. run_ensures() catches it in the `except Exception` block, logs a warning, and returns normally. Init proceeds without surfacing the gate error.
Same scenario with an incompatible required_capabilities entry — init and MCP-boot silently continue.
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_ensures`, add `except StoreIncompatibleError: raise` before the broad `except Exception` handler, so incompatibility propagates to the caller (init, MCP-boot) rather than being swallowed as a sweep no-op. Alternatively, call `check_store_compat()` explicitly at the top of `init_cli` (and any MCP-boot entry) before invoking `run_ensures`, which keeps the gate visible and un-swallowable at each entry point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `_apply_mutations()` gate insertion point precisely: either state 'after the `if not persist` guard that must be added at the top of the function' or 'immediately before the `applier.apply(...)` call at line ~847, unconditionally (persist=False passes reach apply but pass persist=False so applier.apply is a no-op)' — whichever matches the intended semantics.
evidence: Plan: 'Gate there, after the persist=True check (so dry-run / reconcile-check / cap-0 no-write passes are excluded) and before applier.apply()'

reconcile.py _PassContext.persist defaults to True (line 505); within _apply_mutations, persist is checked at lines 846-858 (target_mode kwarg conditional) and lines 867-883 (health.record_pass guard) — no single top-level if-not-persist guard exists
The plan's 'after the persist=True check' cannot be mapped to a single insertion point in the current function body
findings.finding: The plan instructs inserting the gate in `_apply_mutations()` 'after the `persist=True` check', but no such single guard exists in the actual function — `persist` is used inside multiple conditional branches (applier.apply kwarg, health.record_pass guard, nowrite_plan branch) rather than as a top-level `if not persist: return`. An implementer reading the plan against the real code will find no unambiguous placement anchor.
findings.location: What section / Scope bullet: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` — `_apply_mutations()` gate placement; reconcile.py lines 776–910
scenarios: An implementer inserts check_store_compat() before applier.apply() but after the filter narrowing block, accidentally gating dry-run passes that have persist=False but still reach that code path
An implementer adds a new top-level 'if not persist: return' guard to satisfy the plan's wording, changing the function's existing behavior for non-persist passes
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'after the persist=True check' with: 'immediately before the applier.apply() dispatch (the sole call site, ~line 847), which is reached by both persist=True and persist=False paths — the persist flag is already passed into applier.apply(), so the gate fires for all paths but only the persist=True path will actually issue Jira writes; this correctly mirrors the cross-project guard precedent at applier.py:637-652 which also gates before the same dispatch point.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `_apply_mutations()` contains a 'persist=True check' that the new gate should be placed after ("after the `persist=True` check"), but the actual `_apply_mutations()` function does not perform the persist determination — it reads `ctx.persist` (set by `_load_snapshots()`), and the only `persist`-gated logic inside `_apply_mutations()` is in the `finally` block (for `health.record_pass`). The `applier.apply()` call is NOT guarded by a `persist` check inside `_apply_mutations()`; all mutation paths call `applier.apply()` regardless of `ctx.persist`. This means the gating instruction 'after the persist=True check' points to a non-existent guard, and the implementer needs to add an explicit `if not ctx.persist: return` (or `if not persist: return`) check as part of this work, or carefully place the compat check to avoid blocking dry-run.
findings.location: What section / Gate at true structural choke point: 'Gate there, **after the `persist=True` check** ... and before `applier.apply()`'; Scope section: `reconcile._apply_mutations()` entry
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'after the `persist=True` check (so dry-run / reconcile-check / cap-0 no-write passes are excluded) and before `applier.apply()`' with: 'add an explicit `if not ctx.persist: return` guard at the top of `_apply_mutations()`, then immediately call `check_store_compat(ctx.repo_root / ".tickets-tracker")` before `applier.apply()` — so dry-run/reconcile-check/cap-0 paths that never reach `applier.apply()` are excluded.'

findings.finding: The plan places the `check_store_compat()` gate in `reconcile._apply_mutations()` 'after the `persist=True` check' to exclude dry-run/cap-0 passes. However, `_apply_mutations()` does not itself contain a `persist=True` branch check before calling `applier.apply()` — `persist` is already consumed earlier in `_load_snapshots` to set `ctx.persist`. The actual `persist` gating for Jira writes happens inside `applier.apply()` via the `mode`/`persist` kwarg. Placing the gate 'after the `persist=True` check' in `_apply_mutations` requires extracting `persist = ctx.persist` and adding `if not persist: return` before `applier.apply()` — which is implementable but not what the existing code structure makes obvious from the plan's description.
findings.location: Scope section and AC — 'reconcile._apply_mutations() after the persist=True check (before applier.apply())'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'after the persist=True check' with 'after extracting `persist = ctx.persist`; add `if not persist: return` and place `check_store_compat(repo_root / ".tickets-tracker")` immediately before the `if not persist: return` guard so dry-run passes are excluded'.

findings.finding: The plan instructs gating `_apply_mutations()` 'after the `persist=True` check', but no such check wraps `applier.apply()` in that function — `ctx.persist` is only consulted in the `finally` block for `health.record_pass`, not as a pre-call guard on `applier.apply()`. Cap-0 / dry-run exclusion is enforced by the `mode` kwarg inside the applier, not by an explicit guard in `_apply_mutations`. An implementer following the plan literally cannot 'add the gate after the persist check' at that site without first understanding that they must add their own `if not ctx.persist: return` (or equivalent) — or the gate will also fire on dry-run passes, which the plan explicitly says to exclude.
findings.location: What section — 'Gate at the true structural choke point'; Scope — 'reconcile.py — check_store_compat … after the persist=True check'; AC line: 'after the persist=True check (so dry-run / reconcile-check / cap-0 no-write passes are excluded) and before applier.apply()'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'after the persist=True check' with: 'at the very top of `_apply_mutations()`, add `if not ctx.persist: return` to short-circuit no-write passes, then call `check_store_compat(ctx.tracker_dir)` immediately after — so the gate fires only on persisting passes and before any Jira API call.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'persist=True check' exists inside `_apply_mutations()` that can structurally gate the proposed `check_store_compat()` call ('after the `persist=True` check'), but the actual code has no such local branch: `_apply_mutations` reads `ctx.persist` into a local and passes it as a kwarg to `applier.apply(..., persist=persist)` — the persist gate lives inside `applier.apply()`, not as a visible `if persist:` guard in `_apply_mutations`. The plan's specification of where exactly to insert the gate is therefore imprecise and risks the implementer inserting the call at the wrong location (e.g. after `applier.apply()` rather than before it, or outside the effective gating condition).
findings.location: Scope section / 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py — check_store_compat(repo_root/".tickets-tracker") at the top of _apply_mutations() after the persist=True check'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'after the `persist=True` check' with: 'insert `if ctx.persist: check_store_compat(repo_root / ".tickets-tracker")` before the `applier.apply(...)` call at reconcile.py line ~847 — `ctx.persist` is the flag that distinguishes live passes (cap>0) from dry-run/reconcile-check passes (cap=0).'

findings.finding: The plan's AC and 'What' section describe the reconciler gate as placed 'after the persist=True check' inside _apply_mutations(), but _apply_mutations() has no top-level persist guard — ctx.persist is only consumed inside the finally block (health recording). There is no single 'persist=True' check at the top of the function to anchor the gate insertion point. Implementers may insert the gate in the wrong place or need to add a new persist guard themselves.
findings.location: What section: 'Gate there, after the persist=True check … and before applier.apply()' / AC line 3 / src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py:776–902
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'after the persist=True check' with: 'inside _apply_mutations, immediately before the applier.apply() dispatch (around reconcile.py:847), wrapped as `if ctx.persist: check_store_compat(ctx.tracker_dir)` — so cap-0/dry-run passes (persist=False) skip the gate, while live passes are gated before any Jira write.'

findings.finding: The plan's instruction to gate inside `_apply_mutations()` 'after the `persist=True` check' references a branch that does not exist inside that function: `persist` is pre-resolved at `reconcile_once` level (reconcile.py:624-627) and passed in as `ctx.persist` — there is no `if persist:` branch at the top of `_apply_mutations` to place the gate 'after'. An implementer reading the function will not find the named anchor point and must either add a new `if not persist: return` guard first, or use `if persist: check_store_compat(...)`, neither of which is specified.
findings.location: What section — 'Outbound Jira publish' gate; AC line 3 ('at the top of reconcile._apply_mutations() after the persist=True check'); reconcile.py:776-860
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'after the persist=True check' with: 'at the top of _apply_mutations(), guarded as `if not ctx.persist: return  # skip gate on dry-run/no-write` before calling check_store_compat()' — making the guard explicit and self-contained.

findings.finding: `run_ensures` wraps its entire `with write_lock(...)` body (including the `lock.acquire()` call inside `write_lock`) in a broad `except Exception` handler (ensures.py line 161) that logs and swallows ALL exceptions — including `StoreIncompatibleError`. Since the plan gates inside `lock.acquire()`, which is called by `write_lock`, a `StoreIncompatibleError` raised by the compat gate will be caught and silently discarded by `run_ensures`, returning normally as a 'whole-sweep no-op'. This means that when `run_ensures` is called from init, fsck-repair, or MCP-boot against an incompatible store, the fail-closed gate is silently bypassed for the ensure sweep itself.
findings.location: What section — 'Expand phase' and 'Gate at the true structural choke point'; src/rebar/_store/ensures.py lines 148-162

findings.finding: The plan's fail-closed guarantee for corrupt/incompatible records is undermined by run_ensures's broad exception handler: inserting the gate inside lock.acquire() means StoreIncompatibleError raised from acquire() will be caught by run_ensures's `except Exception` (ensures.py:161), logged as a warning, and silently swallowed — making the gate fail-OPEN on the init/MCP-boot path.
findings.location: What > 'Gate at the true structural choke point' section; src/rebar/_store/ensures.py:148-162
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `check_store_compat(tracker)` call in `run_ensures` BEFORE the `try: with write_lock(...)` block (line 148 of ensures.py). Since the gate read is cheap and idempotent, this ensures the incompatibility is surfaced (and propagates to the caller) before the fail-silent lock acquisition attempt swallows it. Alternatively, in the `except Exception` block, add `if isinstance(exc, StoreIncompatibleError): raise` to re-raise it explicitly.

findings.finding: The structured output format (field name and schema) for read-only commands reporting an incompatible or corrupt record is never defined. AC6 instructs verification via `jq -e / an anchored regex` and names 'the unsupported version/capability identifier', but no field name (e.g. `unsupported_capability`, `compat_error`) is specified. An executor must invent the schema to write both the implementation and the test, making the AC partially effort-framed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 ('read-only commands … structured output / stderr (WARNING+) … verified by a test asserting the exact field via `jq -e` / an anchored regex')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section and AC6 a concrete example: `{"compat_error": {"kind": "unknown_capability|unknown_format_version|corrupt_record", "detail": "<string>"}}` and update the AC to: 'assert `.compat_error.kind == "unknown_capability"` via `jq -e`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact insertion point in `_apply_mutations()`: since no top-level `persist` check currently exists before `applier.apply()`, the plan must either (a) specify that the implementer adds `if not ctx.persist: return` before `check_store_compat()`, or (b) relocate the gate to a point where the persist guard already exists (e.g. inside the `if persist:` block in `_persist_and_log`, or at the `reconcile_once` call site after `persist` is resolved in `_load_snapshots`).
evidence: Plan quote: 'after the ``persist=True`` check (so dry-run / reconcile-check / cap-0 no-write passes are excluded) and before ``applier.apply()``'

Actual _apply_mutations() code (lines 776-901): no top-level 'if not persist: return' or 'if persist:' guard exists before the applier.apply() call; ctx.persist is accessed only in the finally block for health recording
persist is set in _load_snapshots (lines 624-627) and passed as a kwarg to applier.apply() (lines 851-859), not checked at the entry of _apply_mutations()

Partially-verified: the persist exclusion mechanism exists in the codebase but NOT at the described insertion point — the dry-run gate the plan relies on is in applier.apply() internals, not in _apply_mutations() itself
findings.finding: The plan asserts the reconcile gate is inserted 'after the persist=True check' at the top of _apply_mutations(), but the actual _apply_mutations() function contains NO top-level persist guard before calling applier.apply() — persist is set on ctx in _load_snapshots and threaded as a kwarg into applier.apply(), with persist-mode branching living in _persist_and_log. The stated insertion point references a code structure that does not exist, so the dry-run/cap-0 exclusion the plan relies on may not be achieved at the described location.
findings.location: What section / Scope section: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py — check_store_compat(repo_root/".tickets-tracker") at the top of _apply_mutations() after the persist=True check'; AC line: 'Gate there, after the persist=True check (so dry-run / reconcile-check / cap-0 no-write passes are excluded) and before applier.apply()'
scenarios: Implementer inserts check_store_compat() at the top of _apply_mutations() and adds 'if not ctx.persist: return' themselves — works but the plan omitted this necessary step, risking the gate firing on dry-run passes

Implementer inserts check_store_compat() without a persist guard, causing StoreIncompatibleError to block read-only reconcile-check / dry-run passes — contradicting the plan's stated dry-run exclusion semantics

findings.finding: The plan instructs inserting check_store_compat() inside reconcile._apply_mutations() 'after the persist=True check,' implying a structural early-exit guard on persist=False exists in that function. In the actual code, _apply_mutations() has no such early-exit branch — it always calls applier.apply(..., persist=persist), delegating the no-write enforcement into the applier. The actionable anchor 'before applier.apply()' (also in the AC) is correct, but the 'after the persist=True check' framing misrepresents the control flow an implementer will actually encounter, and could lead to the gate being placed incorrectly (e.g., after a self-written `if not persist: return` that would inadvertently skip the gate on dry-run/cap-0 passes) or trigger confusion about where exactly to insert.
findings.location: What section / Scope / AC line 3 — 'reconcile._apply_mutations() after the persist=True check (before applier.apply())'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC line 3, replace: 'after the persist=True check (so dry-run / reconcile-check / cap-0 no-write passes are excluded) and before applier.apply()' with: 'at the top of _apply_mutations(), before applier.apply() — add an explicit `if not ctx.persist: return` guard first (none exists today), then call check_store_compat(ctx.repo_root / ".tickets-tracker") after it, so dry-run/cap-0 passes are excluded and live passes are blocked before any Jira write.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the tracker-path citation in the Scope/What section from `reconcile.py:133/670` to `reconcile.py:670` (the only line in `_load_snapshots` that sets `tracker_dir = repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"`).
evidence: Plan text: 'hardcoded at `reconcile.py:133/670`'
reconcile.py line 133: `def _commit_binding_store_snapshot(` — unrelated function, no tracker path
reconcile.py line 670: `tracker_dir = repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"  # tickets-boundary-ok` — the actual location
findings.finding: The plan cites `reconcile.py:133` as a location where the tracker path is hardcoded ('hardcoded at `reconcile.py:133/670`'), but line 133 of reconcile.py is the start of the unrelated `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` function definition — not a tracker path reference. Only line 670 (`tracker_dir = repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"`) is the relevant location. An implementer using the ':133' citation to find the canonical tracker resolution will land in the wrong function.
findings.location: What section, 'Gate at the true structural choke point' bullet: 'Use the reconciler's own tracker path (`repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"`, hardcoded at `reconcile.py:133/670`)'
scenarios: An implementer who follows the ':133' citation to find the canonical tracker path lands in `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` and has no reference for whether there is a second resolution site they must also guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'hardcoded at `reconcile.py:133/670`' with 'hardcoded at `reconcile.py:670` (inside `_load_snapshots`)' — only one location, at line 670.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Place the `check_store_compat()` call in `fsck_recover_cli` after the `--detect-only` / `--help` argument parsing and early-return checks, not at the very top — so detect-only and help invocations remain available under an incompatible record, consistent with the plan's stated read/diagnostic allowance.
evidence: Plan: 'Add an explicit check_store_compat(tracker_dir) at the top of fsck_recover_cli before any mutation'
fsck_recover.py:134: `if detect_only:` — exits at line 151 with return 3, never touching git state
Plan 'Read/diagnostic allowance': 'Under an incompatible/corrupt record, the read-only commands — fsck (diagnostic mode), show, list, search — stay available'
The --detect-only path is the fsck_recover equivalent of fsck's diagnostic mode — it only reads git state, never writes
findings.finding: The plan places the explicit `check_store_compat()` gate 'at the top of `fsck_recover_cli`', but `--detect-only` is a purely diagnostic, non-mutating operation (it exits 3 without touching git state) that would be blocked before it reaches its early-return guard. This contradicts the plan's own read/diagnostic allowance principle ('read-only commands stay available under an incompatible/corrupt record'), which exempts fsck's diagnostic path for exactly this reason.
findings.location: Plan section 'Two paths do NOT go through lock.acquire()' / 'fsck_recover.py'; fsck_recover.py:134-151 (--detect-only early return at line 134)
scenarios: An operator has an incompatible store and wants to check whether the tracker is in stale-rebase state before deciding on recovery. Running `rebar fsck-recover --detect-only` would be blocked by the gate placed at the top of fsck_recover_cli, even though no mutation would occur — defeating the diagnostic exemption the plan explicitly grants.
findings.suggested_fix: Move `check_store_compat(tracker_dir)` to just before the first mutating operation in `fsck_recover_cli` (i.e., after the `if detect_only:` block at line 134 and after the `if not rebase_kind and not recover_dangling: return 0` fast-exit at line 153), so detect-only and no-op paths are ungated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `check_store_compat()` gate in `fsck_recover_cli` is inserted after the `--detect-only` flag is parsed and only on the mutating path (i.e., guarding the `rebase --continue`/`--abort`/cherry-pick block), not at the absolute top of the function — consistent with the plan's read/diagnostic allowance principle.
evidence: Plan: 'Add an explicit check_store_compat(tracker_dir) at the top of fsck_recover_cli before any mutation'
Plan: 'Under an incompatible/corrupt record, the read-only commands — fsck (diagnostic mode), show, list, search — stay available'
fsck_recover.py lines 134-151: the --detect-only branch makes zero git mutations (only reads git-dir marker files) and returns exit 3 — it is a diagnostic, not a mutation
Placing the gate 'at the top' (before the argv parse and detect_only flag is set) blocks --detect-only even though it holds no lock and mutates nothing
findings.finding: The plan says to insert `check_store_compat()` 'at the top of `fsck_recover_cli`' unconditionally, but `fsck_recover_cli` contains a `--detect-only` path (lines 134–151) that is a read-only diagnostic (inspects git state, prints, exits 3) — not a mutation. Blocking it with the gate contradicts the plan's own read/diagnostic allowance principle ('read-only commands — fsck (diagnostic mode), show, list, search — stay available') and would block a useful recovery-diagnostic tool under an incompatible store.
findings.location: What section: 'Two paths do NOT go through lock.acquire()…' bullet 1; fsck_recover.py lines 77–151
scenarios: An operator's store has an incompatible format_version; they run `rebar fsck-recover --detect-only` to check if a stale rebase is present before deciding whether to upgrade — the gate blocks them with a non-zero exit before they can get the diagnostic
An operator needs to know if fsck-recover is needed (detect-only) but cannot run it due to the gate; they are left without a safe way to inspect recovery state
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'at the top of fsck_recover_cli before any mutation' to 'after argv parsing, before the first mutating git call (the `git rebase --continue`/`git merge --abort`/`git cherry-pick` block), skipping the gate when `detect_only=True` — consistent with the diagnostic-allowance principle applied to fsck's own diagnostic mode.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the rollback-test mechanism between the What section ('git-stash the gate code, run rebar fsck') and AC5 ('monkeypatch `check_store_compat` to a no-op') — pick one canonical approach (likely the monkeypatch/interface-test form that can run in CI) and delete or demote the other to a 'developer verification' note.
evidence: What section: 'the hard rollback test below is its empirical proof (git-stash the gate code, run `rebar fsck` against a store containing `.store-compat.json`, assert operable + file byte-unchanged)'

AC5: 'simulate a pre-v1.0 binary by invoking the store layer with the gate patched out (monkeypatch `check_store_compat` to a no-op, emulating code that predates it) against a store carrying the committed `.store-compat.json`'
findings.finding: The What section and AC5 give contradictory implementations of the rollback test. What says the hard rollback test is 'git-stash the gate code, run `rebar fsck` against a store containing `.store-compat.json`' (an integration-level stash-and-run approach), while AC5 says 'simulate a pre-v1.0 binary by invoking the store layer with the gate patched out (monkeypatch `check_store_compat` to a no-op, emulating code that predates it)' — a unit/interface test approach. An implementer following the What section would produce a manual integration test that cannot run in CI; following AC5 they produce a monkeypatched interface test. Both cannot simultaneously be the single 'hard rollback test'.
findings.location: What section — 'Rollback safety (decided, then proven)' paragraph vs. Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (rollback test bullet)
scenarios: Implementer builds a git-stash manual test that cannot run in CI, while the AC verifier expects a monkeypatched interface test — one approach passes CI, the other does not.
Two reviewers could approve the plan referencing different test implementations, causing spec drift.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the What-section parenthetical with: 'empirically proven by the AC5 interface test (monkeypatch `check_store_compat` to a no-op, drive store operations against a store carrying `.store-compat.json`, assert exit 0 + file byte-unchanged).' The git-stash approach can be noted as an optional developer sanity-check, not part of the required test suite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the UPDATE lifecycle for `required_capabilities`: define who (which command/process), when, and via what mechanism writes a non-empty `required_capabilities` list to the committed `.store-compat.json` on the `tickets` branch when a new capability feature ships — and add a corresponding AC verifying an updated record blocks a binary whose `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES` does not include the new entry.
evidence: "KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str] = frozenset() — the set of capability strings this binary understands"
"Future capabilities are added to this set only when the feature that consumes them ships"
No corresponding description of who writes a non-empty required_capabilities list to .store-compat.json and when — only the initial absent→present stamping is specified
AC-1 only verifies the record is "created at init" with no mention of how it is updated when a capability is added
findings.finding: The UPDATE lifecycle for the `required_capabilities` field is not specified: the plan describes only the initial stamping (absent → present with empty list), but never specifies who writes a non-empty `required_capabilities` entry to `.store-compat.json` on the `tickets` branch, when they do so, or what process/command triggers that committed write when a new capability feature ships. Without this path, the gate infrastructure exists but can never be triggered in practice — `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES` stays empty, `required_capabilities` stays `[]`, and the gate never blocks anything beyond format_version mismatches.
findings.location: What section — 'Recognised-capability vocabulary' and 'Scope boundary'; Acceptance Criteria AC-1 and AC-7
scenarios: A future feature ships that requires capability 'compact-v2'; the developer adds 'compact-v2' to KNOWN_CAPABILITIES in the binary but has no specified mechanism to write required_capabilities: ['compact-v2'] to the committed .store-compat.json — old binaries are never blocked.

A developer assumes the ensure unit handles both creation and update, attempts to ship a required capability, but the ensure unit only writes when absent — so existing stores never get the updated record.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection (e.g. 'Adding a required capability') that specifies: (1) the capability string is added to KNOWN_CAPABILITIES in the binary; (2) a separate, intentional store-upgrade command (e.g. `rebar store upgrade --require-capability <name>`) or a migration ensure unit writes the updated record to .store-compat.json and commits it to the tickets branch; (3) the upgrade is a deliberate operator action, not automatic, so old-binary stores are never silently upgraded to block themselves.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the rollback proof: either (a) audit the codebase for every path that reads files at the store root level (not just per-ticket dirs) and confirm none will open `.store-compat.json`, documenting each in the plan, OR (b) add a test that actually exercises a checkout of the pre-v1.0 codebase (or a fixture capturing its file-reading behaviour) against a store containing the new file, rather than a monkeypatch of the gate function.
evidence: Plan: 'the hard rollback test below is its empirical proof (git-stash the gate code, run `rebar fsck` against a store containing `.store-compat.json`, assert operable + file byte-unchanged)'

AC5 test: 'simulates a pre-v1.0 binary by invoking the store layer with the gate patched out (monkeypatch `check_store_compat` to a no-op, emulating code that predates it)' — this proves nothing about whether actual pre-v1.0 code paths open or error on the new file

The plan's name-novelty argument ('pre-v1.0 replay/compaction/fsck open only event files and the known metadata files') is a claim about the absence of pre-v1.0 file reads at this path, which is verifiable from the codebase but the test as described does not verify it
findings.finding: The rollback-safety 'empirical proof' is a monkeypatch simulation (patching `check_store_compat` to a no-op), not a test against actual pre-v1.0 code; the unverified capability assertion that `.store-compat.json` is invisible to ALL pre-v1.0 file-open paths rests only on the name-novelty argument, which is not validated by the proposed test.
findings.location: What section — 'Rollback safety (decided, then proven)' paragraph; AC5
scenarios: If any pre-v1.0 code path does a glob or directory walk that tries to parse `.store-compat.json` as an event file (e.g. any path that iterates `*.json` at the store root rather than inside per-ticket dirs), rollback would silently corrupt or error rather than pass through
The monkeypatch test confirms the v1.0 gate-removed path works, but cannot catch a pre-v1.0 code path that errors on the new file at a different location

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an anchored, AST-based (or at minimum word-boundary-anchored grep) pattern for the coverage lint in AC3b — e.g. use `grep -Pn '\block\.acquire\('` limited to `.py` source files excluding test fixtures, or use an AST import-graph check — and add a brief note on how 'routes through the gate' is asserted (e.g. 'the call-site's enclosing function is `lock.acquire` in `lock.py`, verified by import tracing').
evidence: "a coverage test/lint that greps the tree for **both** `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(` (and asserts each routes through the gate)"
Defect taxonomy item 1: substring false-positive — 'anchor the pattern with a word-boundary / quoted key'
No anchored pattern or import-tracing approach is specified
findings.finding: The coverage/lint test in AC3b is specified as 'greps the tree for both `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(`' but no anchored grep pattern is given — a naive substring search can match occurrences in comments, docstrings, or string literals, producing a false-positive pass that doesn't actually prove coverage.
findings.location: AC3, sub-criterion (b): 'a coverage test/lint that greps the tree for both `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(`'
scenarios: The grep finds `lock.acquire(` in a test fixture comment or a docstring example; the CI lint passes even though a new production call-site was added without the gate.
A future contributor adds `lock.acquire(` in a string literal (e.g. an error message), bumping the count the lint checks — or the lint uses a count that includes non-production hits.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `StoreIncompatibleError` propagated from `_apply_mutations()` is surfaced in the reconciler: add a required structured log line (level ERROR/CRITICAL) and, if the reconciler exposes metrics, a counter increment — and state whether the reconcile loop terminates, skips the pass, or retries on the next cycle.
evidence: Plan says gate 'mirrors the existing fail-closed cross-project guard precedent at applier.py:637-652 (refuse BEFORE issuing any Jira write … abort the whole pass, no partial writes)' but says nothing about logging, metrics, or reconcile-loop continuation behavior when the gate fires.

T5b observability sub-check: 'New failure points instrumented with a metric/log/trace/alert' — the reconciler is described as a per-pass engine whose failures need operator visibility; the plan specifies only that StoreIncompatibleError is raised, not how it is logged or whether it is counted/alerted.
findings.finding: The plan gates the reconciler's `_apply_mutations()` with `check_store_compat()` but does not specify how `StoreIncompatibleError` is surfaced in the long-running reconciler context: no log level, metric increment, or statement about whether the reconcile loop terminates, retries, or continues after the exception propagates.
findings.location: What section / Scope: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` — `_apply_mutations()` gate; AC line: 'Gate there, after the persist=True check … before applier.apply()'
scenarios: Reconciler is deployed in a long-running host; gate fires on every pass after a capability upgrade; operator has no metric/alert and cannot distinguish a gate-blocked pass from a crash or a network failure without reading raw logs.
Gate fires transiently (filesystem blip misread as corrupt record); reconciler silently stops pushing Jira updates with no alarm.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope / AC: 'When `check_store_compat()` raises in `_apply_mutations()`, the reconciler MUST emit a structured ERROR log naming the unsupported capability/version and the store path, increment a `rebar_reconcile_gate_blocked_total` counter (or equivalent), and abort only the current pass (returning/re-raising so the calling loop can apply its existing backoff/retry policy rather than spinning tight).'  Mirror the existing `applier.py:637-652` guard's log pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in `docs/migrations.md` the forward-rollback schema state explicitly: when rolling back from a future binary that has committed non-empty `required_capabilities`, the `.store-compat.json` remains on the tickets branch with an incompatible record, the v1.0 binary fails-closed on all lock-held writes (DATA LOSS RISK: writes blocked until resolved), and the primary operator recovery path is `git -C .tickets-tracker rm .store-compat.json && git -C .tickets-tracker commit -m 'chore: remove compat record for rollback'`; update AC5's rollback test to also assert this forward-rollback schema state and recovery.
evidence: Plan says: 'The delete-.store-compat.json recovery is documented as belt-and-suspenders only'
AC5 rollback test only simulates the pre-v1.0 (gate-patched) scenario, not the v1.0-rolling-back-from-post-v1.0-required-capability scenario
No explicit statement of schema state when a v1.0 binary encounters required_capabilities=["some-future-cap"] after rolling back from a future binary
T11 POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE requirement: plan must assert on schema STATE after rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0; where destructiveness is ambiguous, attach a literal DATA LOSS RISK note
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the schema state and primary recovery procedure after a forward rollback where a post-v1.0 binary has already written non-empty `required_capabilities` into the committed `.store-compat.json`. In that scenario a v1.0 binary encounters an unknown capability and fails-closed — the store is effectively read-only — yet the plan labels 'delete `.store-compat.json`' as 'belt-and-suspenders only' rather than the primary documented operator recovery path, and does not attach a DATA LOSS RISK note or specify what the schema state looks like post-rollback in that case.
findings.location: Plan section 'Rollback safety (decided, then proven)' and AC5; docs/migrations.md scope item
scenarios: Team ships v1.1 that adds a required capability, writes it into the store. A rollback to v1.0 is needed. v1.0 binary reads the record, finds unknown required capability, fails-closed on ALL lock-held mutations. Operator must know to delete .store-compat.json to recover, but the plan documents this as 'belt-and-suspenders' rather than the primary escape hatch.

In a multi-host setup, one host rolls back to v1.0 while another host's store already carries a post-v1.0 required_capabilities record — the v1.0 host is permanently locked out of writes until the record is removed or downgraded.
findings.suggested_fix: In `docs/migrations.md`, add a 'Forward-rollback recovery' section that explicitly names `delete + git commit .store-compat.json` as the PRIMARY escape hatch (not belt-and-suspenders), with a DATA LOSS RISK callout: 'writes are blocked until the record is removed.' Add a second rollback test scenario to AC5 that (a) writes a record with `required_capabilities=["future-cap"]`, (b) runs a v1.0 binary (gate still active, KNOWN_CAPABILITIES empty), (c) asserts fail-closed on mutations, and (d) asserts that after deleting the record the binary operates normally.

findings.finding: The coverage lint / CI check described in AC3 says it 'greps the tree for both `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(`… and asserts each routes through the gate', but does not specify what grep pattern or assertion proves 'routes through the gate'. Since the gate is placed *inside* `lock.acquire()` in `lock.py`, the meaningful assertion is that `lock.py`'s `acquire` method itself calls `check_store_compat` — not a per-call-site grep. Without a concrete check, this CI gate may be written in a way that gives a false green (e.g. grepping for the string `lock.acquire(` across the tree proves nothing about the gate being inside `acquire()`).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('a coverage test/lint that greps the tree for both `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(`… asserting each routes through the gate')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC3 lint description with: 'A lint test asserts (a) `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`'s `acquire` method body contains a call to `check_store_compat` (grep -n "check_store_compat" src/rebar/_store/lock.py returns a match inside the acquire function); (b) `src/rebar/_commands/fsck_recover.py` contains `check_store_compat` before the first `_git(` call; (c) `reconcile.py`'s `_apply_mutations` contains `check_store_compat` before `applier.apply(`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a capability-naming convention (format, namespacing, versioning rules, at least one illustrative example) to `docs/migrations.md` or a companion ADR, so the first real capability addition has a canonical template to follow.
evidence: 'Future capabilities are added to this set only when the feature that consumes them ships (git extensions.* semantics)' — references git semantics without specifying the project's own naming rules
ABSENCE: `docs/migrations.md` spec lists the record, four states, and rollback state but does not list a naming/governance section for capability strings
The initial set is empty, so the first future developer to add a capability has no canonical example or convention to follow
findings.finding: The plan defines `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str] = frozenset()` and notes capabilities are added 'only when the feature that consumes them ships', but provides no documented naming convention, governance rules, or ADR for capability strings — leaving future developers without guidance on format, namespacing, or versioning of capability identifiers.
findings.location: 'Recognised-capability vocabulary' bullet; Scope section — new module under `src/rebar/_store/`
scenarios: Two independently developed features add capabilities with inconsistent naming (`snake_case` vs `kebab-case`, versioned vs unversioned), making the capability set hard to reason about as it grows.
A capability name collides with or is confused with an event-schema type name, obscuring the distinct roles of `required_capabilities` vs. `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to `docs/migrations.md` specifying the capability string format (e.g. `<feature-area>/<descriptor>@<version>` or a simpler `kebab-case` convention), with a note that it mirrors git's `extensions.<name>` pattern but scoped to rebar's store.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the coverage-test grep to match only `rebar._store.lock.acquire(` (or equivalent import alias patterns like `from rebar._store import lock` + `lock.acquire(`) rather than the bare substring `lock.acquire(`, to avoid false-positive matches on unrelated `.acquire()` calls such as `_advisory_lock.py`'s ref-lock.
evidence: Search result: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_advisory_lock.py:143: return ref_lock.acquire(` — this is a different object's .acquire() call, not rebar._store.lock.acquire
Plan: 'A coverage test/lint greps the tree for both `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(` (and asserts each routes through the gate)'
The grep is a substring match on `lock.acquire(` which matches `ref_lock.acquire(` as a suffix
findings.finding: The plan's proposed CI coverage test greps for `lock.acquire(` across the tree to assert every acquisition is gated, but `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_advisory_lock.py:143` contains `ref_lock.acquire(` — a completely different lock object (a git ref-lock for the reconciler pass, not `rebar._store.lock.acquire`). The grep will produce a false positive, either causing the coverage test to wrongly assert this line needs the compat gate (and fail CI spuriously when it doesn't have it) or requiring an ad-hoc exclusion that weakens the test's stated guarantee.
findings.location: Plan AC section: 'a coverage test/lint that greps the tree for both `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(`'; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_advisory_lock.py:143
scenarios: The coverage test greps for `lock.acquire(` and finds _advisory_lock.py:143. It then asserts that line imports or calls check_store_compat. It doesn't — causing a spurious CI failure that reveals the grep approach is too broad.
findings.suggested_fix: In the coverage test, import-graph the callers of `rebar._store.lock.acquire` (or grep for `from rebar._store import lock` + `lock.acquire(` / `from rebar._store.lock import acquire`) rather than the plain substring, or explicitly enumerate the modules that are allowed to call lock.acquire() and assert each has the gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'e.g. `.store-compat.json`' with the committed, canonical path name (e.g. state: 'The fixed path IS `.store-compat.json`') and repeat that name verbatim in AC1 so executor and tests share one source of truth.
evidence: "Add a committed store-level record...at a fixed store path (e.g. `.store-compat.json`)"
AC1: 'created at init at a fixed path' — path name not pinned in the AC
findings.finding: The store path is stated as 'e.g. `.store-compat.json`' in the What section and as 'at a fixed store path' in AC1, leaving the actual committed path as an example rather than a decided value — an executor could choose a different name and satisfy the letter of both.
findings.location: What section ('e.g. `.store-compat.json`'); AC1 ('at a fixed store path')
scenarios: Executor chooses '.compat.json' or 'store-format.json'; downstream docs, tests, and grep assertions all use different names; integration tests diverge.
findings.suggested_fix: In both the What section and AC1, replace 'e.g. `.store-compat.json`' / 'at a fixed store path' with 'at the committed path `.store-compat.json`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the `SCHEMA_VERSION`/`format_version` relationship is documented — e.g. 'a docstring or module-level comment in `reducer/_version.py` states that `SCHEMA_VERSION` is the event-schema discriminator and `format_version` is the store-format gate, and that they move independently; verified by a grep/AST check in CI'.
evidence: Scope bullet: 'Clarify the relationship to `SCHEMA_VERSION` in `reducer/_version.py` (it stays the event-schema discriminator; `format_version` is the new store-format gate — state whether they move together or independently)'
No AC references `_version.py`, `SCHEMA_VERSION`, or the relationship between the two versioning mechanisms
findings.finding: The Scope section lists 'Clarify the relationship to `SCHEMA_VERSION` in `reducer/_version.py`' as a deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion maps to it — an executor can deliver every AC and skip this item without any CI or review signal catching the omission.
findings.location: Scope section, bullet: 'src/rebar/reducer/_version.py — relate `format_version` to the existing preserve-and-ignore `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` machinery and to `SCHEMA_VERSION`'
scenarios: Executor delivers all 8 ACs; `_version.py` is left unmodified with no clarifying comment; a future engineer conflates SCHEMA_VERSION with format_version, either redundantly gating on both or bypassing one.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '`reducer/_version.py` contains a module-level comment (or docstring) explicitly stating `SCHEMA_VERSION` is the per-event schema discriminator, `format_version` is the store-format gate, and they move independently — verified by `grep -q "move independently" src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` in the lint suite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion (or extend AC7/AC8) that explicitly verifies the `reducer/_version.py` relationship: e.g. 'A comment or docstring in `_version.py` states whether `format_version` and `SCHEMA_VERSION` move together or independently, and the AC is verifiable by a code-review spot-check of that file.'
evidence: Scope: 'src/rebar/reducer/_version.py — relate `format_version` to the existing preserve-and-ignore `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` machinery and to `SCHEMA_VERSION` (it stays the event-schema discriminator; `format_version` is the new store-format gate — state whether they move together or independently)'
No AC references `_version.py`, `SCHEMA_VERSION`, or the stated/documented relationship between the two versioning concepts.
findings.finding: The Scope section lists `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` as a deliverable ('relate `format_version` to the existing preserve-and-ignore `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` machinery and to `SCHEMA_VERSION`… state whether they move together or independently'), but no Acceptance Criterion covers this change. No AC verifies that `_version.py` was updated, that the relationship between `format_version` and `SCHEMA_VERSION` was documented, or that the preserve-and-ignore machinery was left intact in that module.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` bullet; Acceptance Criteria (all AC bullets)
scenarios: Implementer skips the `_version.py` update (it has no failing test or AC check), and a future developer conflates `SCHEMA_VERSION` with `format_version` causing incorrect gate logic.
Reviewer cannot close the AC checklist against the `_version.py` change because no AC names it.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC8 (docs bullet) or add a new AC: '`src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` contains an explicit comment or docstring clarifying that `SCHEMA_VERSION` is the event-schema discriminator (preserve-and-ignore) and `format_version` is the store-format gate (fail-closed), and states whether the two version values are advanced together or independently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the `.tickets-tracker` literal into a single named constant (e.g. `DEFAULT_TRACKER_SUBDIR` in a shared location) and have both `reconcile.py` and the `check_store_compat()` call-site derive the path from that constant — do not introduce a third independent hardcoding; note this in the Scope.
evidence: Plan says the reconciler hardcodes `.tickets-tracker` at lines 133 and 670 — the gate call is therefore a third site hardcoding the same string with no shared constant.

T5e changeability sub-check: 'Rules/thresholds expected to evolve are configurable, not hardcoded' — tracker directory location is noted as an internal constant in at least two existing places; a third independent hardcoding is a changeability risk.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly directs hardcoding `repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"` in the reconciler gate call (rather than using `config.tracker_dir()`) because the reconciler resolves the store directly at that literal path — but this makes the gate's store-path assumption a silent duplicate of the reconciler's own internal constant, so if the tracker directory is ever renamed or made configurable the gate call will silently check the wrong (possibly absent) path and pass through instead of gating.
findings.location: What section / Scope: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py`; plan prose: 'Use the reconciler's own tracker path (repo_root / ".tickets-tracker", hardcoded at reconcile.py:133/670), NOT config.tracker_dir()'
scenarios: Tracker directory is renamed or made configurable in a future PR; the gate's literal `".tickets-tracker"` string is not updated; gate checks a non-existent path; absent-record = pass-through silently bypasses the gate for all reconcile passes.
A test environment mounts the store at a custom path; the reconciler resolves it correctly via its own constant but the gate checks the wrong hardcoded path, giving a false pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Extract the `".tickets-tracker"` literal from `reconcile.py:133/670` into a module-level constant (e.g. `_store.TRACKER_SUBDIR = ".tickets-tracker"`); update reconcile.py to use it; pass `repo_root / _store.TRACKER_SUBDIR` to `check_store_compat()` — so there is one definition, not three.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the TOCTOU between `check_store_compat()` and fcntl lock acquisition in `lock.acquire()`, and either (a) re-read and re-check the record after the fcntl lock is held (double-checked locking), or (b) explicitly document this as an accepted residual risk given the narrow window and the fact that the ensure unit only writes compatible records.
evidence: "Insert the check_store_compat() gate inside lock.acquire(), after canonical_tracker() resolves the path and before the fcntl/mkdir legs"
The fcntl/mkdir legs are the actual OS-level exclusion mechanism — the compat check explicitly precedes them
The atomic rename for writing the record does not synchronize with the compat check in a concurrent reader's lock.acquire()
findings.finding: The `check_store_compat()` call inside `lock.acquire()` runs before the OS-level fcntl lock is held (the plan says 'after `canonical_tracker()` resolves the path and before the fcntl/mkdir legs'), creating a narrow TOCTOU window: two concurrent processes could both pass the compat check, then one could write an incompatible record (via the ensure unit or an external tool) before the second process acquires the lock — allowing the second to proceed with an incompatible store.
findings.location: What section — 'Gate at the true structural choke point'; Scope — `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`
scenarios: Process A: lock.acquire() → check passes (record absent, pass-through) → yield to OS → Process B: ensure unit writes .store-compat.json with format_version=99 → Process A resumes, acquires fcntl lock, proceeds with incompatible store.

More realistically: the ensure unit in one process races with the gate check in another during the brief window between check and fcntl acquisition — low probability but non-zero in concurrent MCP-boot scenarios.
findings.suggested_fix: After acquiring the fcntl lock (inside the locked section), perform a second `check_store_compat()` read to close the TOCTOU — the first pre-lock check is a fast-path optimization; the post-lock check is the definitive gate. This mirrors double-checked locking and adds one extra file read on the hot path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC3 sub-case: write a record with `{"format_version": 2, "required_capabilities": []}` and assert that a lock-held mutation, an `fsck_recover` invocation, and a reconcile pass are each blocked with `StoreIncompatibleError` (store files byte-unchanged, zero Jira calls) — covering the format_version trigger independently of the required_capabilities trigger.
evidence: AC3 test spec: 'writing a record with a required_capabilities entry ∉ KNOWN_CAPABILITIES (e.g. ["unknown-v99"])'
Gate semantics state 'unknown format_version OR a required_capabilities entry ∉ KNOWN_CAPABILITIES → fail-closed' (Four record states, state 3)
No AC mentions a test with format_version set to an unknown integer value
findings.finding: The plan defines two independent fail-closed triggers — (a) unknown `format_version` integer and (b) a `required_capabilities` entry ∉ `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES` — but AC3's test specification only drives the required_capabilities path (writing `["unknown-v99"]`). No AC explicitly requires a test that writes a record with `format_version: 2` (or any future unknown integer) and asserts the gate fires and mutations are blocked. The unknown-format_version trigger therefore has no behavioral test coverage mandated by the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (gate test spec) and 'Four record states' / 'The gate error' in the What section
scenarios: A future store writer sets format_version=2 to signal a structural change; a v1.0 binary processes it without blocking because the format_version branch of the gate was never tested and a subtle implementation bug only covers the required_capabilities branch.
CI passes green on both branches but the format_version gate is silently broken.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, extend the test scenario to: '(a-i) record with required_capabilities ["unknown-v99"] and (a-ii) record with format_version 2 and empty required_capabilities — both must block all three mutation paths with StoreIncompatibleError.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that shipping `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES = frozenset()` (zero members, zero consumers at v1.0 launch) is deliberate and justified by the deployment-ordering constraint, and document this explicitly in the new module's docstring and in `docs/migrations.md` — so future contributors understand the empty vocabulary is intentional, not an oversight.
evidence: 'KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str] = frozenset() — the set of capability strings this binary understands … Future capabilities are added to this set only when the feature that consumes them ships'
No capability string is defined anywhere in the plan or codebase today; codebase search for 'KNOWN_CAPABILITIES' returns zero hits
At launch every store will have `required_capabilities: []`; the gate branch `if any entry ∉ KNOWN_CAPABILITIES` can never fire until a post-v1.0 binary writes a non-empty list
findings.finding: `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str] = frozenset()` is an empty vocabulary at launch — no capability string is defined, no existing feature requires any, so the capability-checking branch of the gate is dead code at v1.0. The Rule-of-Three criterion requires an abstraction to have ≥3 existing call-sites or serve a current done-definition; here the capability set has zero members and zero consumers at launch.
findings.location: What section — 'Recognised-capability vocabulary'; Scope — 'New module … exporting KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str]'
scenarios: At v1.0 launch the capability-checking code path is unreachable by any store in existence, meaning it is unexercised in production and only tested by synthetic fixtures.
If the post-v1.0 feature that would populate KNOWN_CAPABILITIES is never shipped, the vocabulary mechanism remains dead indefinitely.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring paragraph to the new `_store/compat.py` module (and a sentence in `docs/migrations.md`) that reads: 'KNOWN_CAPABILITIES is intentionally empty at v1.0. The reader gate must ship before any writer can populate required_capabilities; the vocabulary is expected to grow only when a specific post-v1.0 feature requires it. An empty set means the capability check can never block on any current store — this is correct and expected at v1.0 launch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the coverage lint to grep only `lock.acquire(` direct callers (the non-context-manager callers that can bypass `write_lock(`) and confirm `fsck_recover_cli` + `_apply_mutations` call `check_store_compat`; drop the separate `write_lock(` grep since `write_lock()` unconditionally delegates to `acquire()` and is already covered by the single insertion point.
evidence: lock.py:316: `handle = acquire(tracker, timeout=timeout, attempts=attempts, dual_window=dual_window)` — write_lock() is a one-line wrapper over acquire()
Plan states: 'greps the tree for both lock.acquire( and write_lock( (and asserts each routes through the gate)'

Grepping write_lock( call-sites and asserting they route through the gate is equivalent to asserting Python function calls work — the single insertion point in acquire() already guarantees this structurally
findings.finding: The coverage lint is over-specified for `write_lock(` callers: the plan requires CI to grep `write_lock(` and 'assert each routes through the gate', but `write_lock()` is a thin wrapper that unconditionally calls `acquire()` (lock.py:316: `handle = acquire(…)`), so this assertion is vacuously true by construction — any new `write_lock` call-site automatically routes through the gate. The lint adds CI surface area and maintenance burden for a guarantee already enforced architecturally.
findings.location: What section — 'Completeness is CI-enforced'; AC — 'a coverage test/lint greps the tree for both lock.acquire( and write_lock('
scenarios: A new `write_lock(` call-site is added; the CI lint flags it even though it already routes through the gate by construction, causing a false-positive failure that wastes developer time.
The lint test for write_lock( callers must be updated every time a new legitimate write_lock call-site is added, creating maintenance overhead.
findings.suggested_fix: In the coverage test, replace the two-grep approach with: (1) assert `lock.acquire` is only ever defined once (in lock.py) and always calls `check_store_compat` before returning; (2) assert `fsck_recover_cli` contains `check_store_compat`; (3) assert `_apply_mutations` contains `check_store_compat`. Drop the `write_lock(` enumeration grep.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` (line 269, `write_lock`) to the Scope section's enumeration of lock-based mutation sites so the coverage lint and implementors have a complete picture.
evidence: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:269: with _store_lock.write_lock(str(tracker_dir), attempts=1, dual_window=True):
Plan scope lists txn.py, compact.py, fsck.py:419 as direct lock.acquire() callers but does not name inbound_translate.py
Plan's CI lint says 'greps the tree for both lock.acquire( and write_lock(' — inbound_translate.py would show up in that grep but is not discussed
findings.finding: inbound_translate.py (src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:269) calls write_lock() directly on the tracker and is an unenumerated lock-based store mutation site outside the _commands/ directory. Although it routes through acquire() and would be covered by the gate, it is not listed in the Scope section or the coverage lint description, which risks it being missed if the lint test only enumerates the files explicitly named.
findings.location: Scope section / 'Completeness is CI-enforced' bullet
scenarios: If a developer reads the Scope section to understand which files need the gate, inbound_translate.py is invisible — creating a false sense that only _commands/ files are affected.
If the coverage lint is written to assert a whitelist of known write_lock( sites, inbound_translate.py must be on that list or the lint will false-fail.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet in the Scope section: '`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` — uses `write_lock()` directly (covered by the `acquire()` gate; enumerate in the lint allowlist)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all remaining `write_lock()` call sites (`sync.py:173`, `push.py:186`, `event_append.py:255`/`:358`, `ensures.py:149`) in the Scope section or the lint description so the CI coverage test has a complete, auditable allowlist.
evidence: src/rebar/_store/sync.py:173: with _lock.write_lock(tracker, timeout=lock_timeout, attempts=1, dual_window=True):
src/rebar/_store/push.py:186: with _lock.write_lock(base_path, ...)
src/rebar/_store/event_append.py:255 and :358: with _lock.write_lock(tracker, dual_window=True):
Plan states 'A coverage test/lint greps the tree for both lock.acquire( and write_lock(' but does not enumerate all sites the grep will find
findings.finding: sync.py, push.py, and event_append.py (two sites) are write_lock() callers in src/rebar/_store/ that are unenumerated in the Scope and the coverage lint description. They are covered by the acquire() gate, but the plan's enumeration of 'all write_lock() callers the lint must cover' is incomplete, which could result in a lint test that fails to assert coverage over these sites or, conversely, a lint whitelist that is written narrowly and false-fails when these sites appear in grep output.
findings.location: Scope section / 'Completeness is CI-enforced' bullet / AC line 3
scenarios: Implementor writing the lint test must enumerate all write_lock( sites — without this list in the plan they must discover them independently, risking an incomplete lint.
A narrow lint that only checks the files named in the Scope section would miss these _store/ callers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/releasing.md` to the leaf's Scope and ACs with a specific note (e.g. a pre-ship checklist entry) documenting that the store-compatibility gate must be present in the tree shipped as v1.0, to satisfy parent AC5's explicit requirement that both docs cover the store compatibility record.
evidence: Parent AC5: '`docs/releasing.md` and `docs/migrations.md` are updated to describe the new release-authorization gate, the build-once artifact flow, and the store compatibility record (including the absent-record upgrade/rollback path).'
Leaf Scope: '`docs/migrations.md` — document the record, the four states, the fail-closed semantics, the absent-record upgrade path, and the concrete rollback state.' (no docs/releasing.md entry)

Parent plan: '`docs/releasing.md` is edited by all three release stories' — referring to findings 1/3/4, leaving the store-compat-record clause of AC5 unassigned to those stories, making this leaf the only vehicle for it.
ABSENCE: `docs/releasing.md` does not appear anywhere in the leaf's Scope, ACs, or file_impact list.
findings.finding: The leaf's Scope and ACs include only `docs/migrations.md` for store-compat documentation, omitting `docs/releasing.md`. Parent AC5 explicitly names both documents as needing to cover the store compatibility record (including the absent-record upgrade/rollback path). Since the parent assigns the store-compat-record clause of AC5 solely to Finding 2 (the release-gate and build-once clauses go to findings 1/3/4), the leaf is the only vehicle for this deliverable, and its omission leaves parent AC5 undeliverable.
findings.location: Leaf Scope section and ACs (no mention of docs/releasing.md); Parent AC5
scenarios: A release engineer following the docs/releasing.md runbook has no pointer to the store compatibility record requirement as a v1.0 pre-ship gate, making it easy to ship without confirming the gate is present.
Parent AC5 cannot be marked done because one of its two named docs was never updated by any child story.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the leaf's Scope section: '`docs/releasing.md` — add a pre-ship checklist entry noting that the store-compatibility gate (`check_store_compat` wired into `lock.acquire()`, `fsck_recover_cli`, and `reconcile._apply_mutations()`) must be present in the tree before tagging v1.0, since it cannot be added retroactively.' Mirror this in a leaf AC bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the What section (and/or Scope) whether `show`, `list`, and `search` call `lock.acquire()` — if they do not, say so (extending the structural-exemption rationale beyond fsck diagnostic); if any do, specify whether those callers must be exempted from the gate (requiring the `read_only` flag the plan explicitly rejected, or a different mechanism).
evidence: 'Gate ALL `lock.acquire()` unconditionally — no `read_only` parameter needed.'
'the read-only commands — fsck (diagnostic mode), show, list, search — stay available (exit 0)'
Structural exemption stated only for fsck diagnostic: 'fsck's diagnostic path reads ticket data without acquiring the lock at all, so it is inherently ungated'
No equivalent assertion for show/list/search lock behaviour anywhere in plan
findings.finding: The plan states 'Gate ALL `lock.acquire()` unconditionally' but also states that read-only commands `show`, `list`, and `search` 'stay available (exit 0)' under an incompatible/corrupt record. The structural exemption rationale is only established for `fsck` diagnostic ('reads ticket data without acquiring the lock at all'). The plan never asserts that `show`, `list`, and `search` similarly hold no lock. If any of these commands do call `lock.acquire()` (e.g. a read-advisory lock), the 'unconditional gate' would block them, directly contradicting the 'stay available' guarantee.
findings.location: What section — 'Gate at the true structural choke point' paragraph AND 'Read/diagnostic allowance' paragraph; AC6 (read-only commands bullet)
scenarios: show/list/search acquire an advisory or shared lock → they are gated → they exit non-zero under an incompatible record → AC6 test fails at CI.
Implementer must audit lock usage for show/list/search manually, with no plan guidance — risk of incorrect implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Gate at the true structural choke point' paragraph: 'The read commands `show`, `list`, and `search` do not call `lock.acquire()` [or: hold only a read-advisory lock that bypasses the store-compat path — specify]; they are therefore structurally exempt identically to `fsck` diagnostic.' This proves the consistency and closes the contradiction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either define 'School B' inline (e.g. 'a check-then-idempotent-write unit, as opposed to a destructive/reset unit') or remove the parenthetical and rely solely on 'idempotent ensure unit' throughout, ensuring terminology is consistent across all sections.
evidence: 'An idempotent ensure unit (School B) writes the record at the current version when absent, at init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot.'
No other occurrence of 'School B' in the plan; no definition or cross-reference provided.
Scope section uses 'the ensure unit that writes the record when absent' — no 'School B'.
findings.finding: The What section uses the term 'School B' exactly once ('An idempotent ensure unit (School B) writes the record…') without definition or cross-reference. This is unexplained jargon that appears in no other section — Scope, AC, and docs all say 'ensure unit' or 'idempotent ensure unit' without the 'School B' qualifier. An implementer unfamiliar with the internal taxonomy cannot determine what 'School B' means or whether it has any implementation consequence.
findings.location: What section — 'Expand phase (writing the record…)' paragraph
scenarios: Implementer implements a 'School A' or differently-structured ensure unit, having no reference for what 'School B' requires; subtle behavioural differences may follow.
Reviewer cannot verify 'School B' compliance because it is undefined.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(School B)' with a brief inline definition such as '(check-then-write: only writes when the record is absent, never overwrites an existing record)' and remove the unexplained taxonomy label.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit every direct caller of `lock.acquire()` (`txn.py._acquire_write_lock`, `compact.py:167`/`:415`, `fsck.py:419`) and every `except`/`finally` block in their lock-acquisition paths to confirm `StoreIncompatibleError` propagates unobstructed to the entry-point handler; add a narrow re-raise or a test that plants the exception inside each intermediate caller and asserts it surfaces at the CLI exit-code level.
evidence: 'entry-point callers convert it to the existing CommandError / non-zero exit path (as other gate errors do)' — this assumes unobstructed propagation through intermediate layers, but those layers are not audited

The plan lists `txn.py`'s `_acquire_write_lock`, `compact.py:167/:415`, `fsck.py:419` as direct callers of `lock.acquire()` — each may have its own exception handling that predates `StoreIncompatibleError`
ABSENCE: no mention of auditing or modifying intermediate exception handlers to let `StoreIncompatibleError` bubble up
findings.finding: The plan inserts `StoreIncompatibleError` into `lock.acquire()` and says 'entry-point callers convert it to CommandError', but it does not audit or guarantee that intermediate callers of `lock.acquire()` — specifically `txn.py`'s `_acquire_write_lock`, `compact.py:167`/`:415`, and `fsck.py:419` — propagate the new exception rather than swallowing it in an existing `except` clause.
findings.location: Scope section / 'Gate at the true structural choke point' bullet; `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`; `txn.py`, `compact.py`, `fsck.py`
scenarios: A broad `except Exception` or `except LockTimeout` in `txn.py`'s write-lock acquisition loop silently swallows `StoreIncompatibleError`, allowing a mutation to proceed against an incompatible store — exactly the fail-closed guarantee the gate is meant to provide.
A `finally` block in `compact.py` or `fsck.py` that calls `lock.release()` on any exception continues as if the gate had not fired, with the caller receiving a confusing error or no error at all.
findings.suggested_fix: Make `StoreIncompatibleError` a subclass of a dedicated base that no existing except clause catches (e.g. `StoreGateError(BaseException)` — not `Exception`), or explicitly document and test the propagation path through each intermediate caller by planting the exception in a unit test that wraps the full call stack.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
